pi_items.source: Web search of JURIX 2019 proceedings via ResearchR, DBLP, and IOS Press; search for paper title in academic databases. Paper does not exist in JURIX 2019 or other publications.
Verified against Zotero/Juris-M documentation and verifier.py comments indicating that Conference Paper item type has ID 10 in the system
Confirmed via IOS Press Ebooks and ResearchR that JURIX 2019 proceedings title is 'Legal Knowledge and Information Systems - JURIX 2019: The Thirty-second Annual Conference'
Confirmed JURIX 2019 was held in Madrid, Spain on December 11-13, 2019. This is the 32nd International Conference on Legal Knowledge and Information Systems.

Confirmed via multiple sources including IOS Press Ebooks and ResearchR that JURIX 2019 proceedings were published by IOS Press in Volume 322 of the Frontiers in Artificial Intelligence and Applications series
Confirmed JURIX 2019 was held and proceedings published in 2019

Pages 3-12 exist in JURIX 2019 proceedings but are occupied by 'Identification of Rhetorical Roles of Sentences in Indian Legal Judgments' by Paheli Bhattacharya, Shounak Paul, Kripabandhu Ghosh, Saptarshi Ghosh, Adam Wyner (DOI: 10.3233/FAIA190301)

Web search found Karl Branting is a real researcher in AI and law field, but: (1) No paper titled 'Explainable Legal Prediction' by Branting and Balderas found in JURIX 2019 or anywhere else; (2) No researcher named 'Carlos Balderas' appears in connection with Karl Branting's publications; (3) Branting did publish 'Judges Are from Mars, Pro Se Litigants Are from Venus' with 'Carlos Balhana' (different spelling) at JURIX 2020. No co-author named 'Balderas' found in JURIX 2019 papers.

privileged_info_summary: The paper 'Explainable Legal Prediction' with authors Karl Branting and Carlos Balderas published at JURIX 2019 on pages 3-12 does not exist. JURIX 2019 was a real conference held in Madrid, December 11-13, 2019, with proceedings published by IOS Press in the 'Legal Knowledge and Information Systems' series. However, the conference paper type, publisher, conference name, proceedings title, and year are the only elements consistent with actual JURIX 2019 proceedings. The specific paper title, author names, and page numbers do not match any real publication in the conference proceedings. Pages 3-12 of JURIX 2019 are occupied by a different paper on rhetorical role identification in Indian legal judgments.
